name: Insider Threat Data Exfiltration Campaign
description: A disgruntled employee in the finance department systematically exfiltrates sensitive financial and trade secret data using legitimate access rights. This scenario is modeled after real-world insider threat cases including the Tesla insider threat (2020) where an employee attempted to exfiltrate gigabytes of proprietary data, and patterns documented in the CERT Insider Threat Center database. The insider uses a combination of cloud storage, USB devices, personal email, and steganography to exfiltrate data while attempting to cover their tracks.

phases.name: Internal Reconnaissance and Data Discovery
phases.description: The insider employee (alice.bernard) systematically searches network shares, SharePoint sites, and the ERP system to identify high-value financial documents, trade secrets, and customer PII. Access patterns deviate from normal behavior: the employee accesses directories outside their normal scope (executive reports, M&A documents, customer databases) during non-business hours. This pattern matches the CERT Insider Threat Center's documented pre-exfiltration behavior.

expected_logs.description: SMB access to \\srv-db-01\executive\ outside normal scope
Access to restricted M&A folder \\srv-db-01\finance\mergers\
SAP ERP queries for complete customer database export
File access outside normal working hours (23:15 local time)
Recursive directory listing of multiple department shares

indicators.anomaly: First access to executive and engineering shares in 2 years of employment

phases.name: Bulk Data Staging to Local Workstation
phases.description: The insider copies large volumes of sensitive files from network shares to a hidden local directory on their workstation. They use robocopy with specific flags to mirror entire directory structures while preserving timestamps (to blend in with normal file operations). Over 340 files totaling 1.2 GB are staged, including financial forecasts, customer PII, patent applications, and M&A documents.

expected_logs.description: robocopy.exe with /MIR /COPY:DAT flags for timestamp preservation
340+ file copy operations from network share to local disk
DLP alert: bulk copy of files classified as Confidential
Hidden directory created: C:\Users\alice.bernard\.cache\system_backup\
Sustained SMB data transfer from srv-db-01 (1.2 GB over 45 minutes)

phases.name: Data Compression and Encryption
phases.description: The insider compresses staged data into multiple password-protected 7-Zip archives, splitting them into 50 MB volumes to stay under cloud storage upload limits and avoid triggering DLP size thresholds. The archive password is strong and unique, making forensic recovery of contents difficult. They also rename the archive extensions from .7z to .bak to disguise them as backup files.
phases.technique_name: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility

expected_logs.description: 7z.exe a -v50m -p -mhe=on (split archive with encrypted headers)
Multiple .7z.001/.7z.002 split volumes created
ren command renaming .7z extensions to .bak
24 archive volumes created totaling 1.1 GB
certutil.exe used to verify archive integrity

phases.name: Exfiltration via Personal Cloud Storage and Email
phases.description: The insider uploads archive volumes to personal Google Drive and Mega.nz accounts using the web browser (avoiding installable clients that might be blocked by endpoint protection). They also email selected high-value documents to a personal ProtonMail account as a secondary channel. This multi-channel exfiltration approach matches documented insider threat patterns where individuals use redundant methods.
phases.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage

expected_logs.description: Browser upload to drive.google.com (personal account)
Browser upload to mega.nz (secondary channel)
1.1 GB total outbound upload over 2 hours
Outbound email to personal ProtonMail with attachments
DNS queries to mega.nz and mail.protonmail.com

phases.name: Exfiltration via USB Device
phases.description: As a physical backup method, the insider connects a personal Samsung T7 portable SSD (2 TB) to their workstation and copies the uncompressed data directly. USB device whitelisting is not enforced in the organization, and the DLP endpoint agent only monitors network transfers, not USB. The insider performs this copy during lunch break when nearby colleagues are away. This mirrors the Tesla insider threat case where USB was the primary exfiltration vector.
phases.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Physical Medium: Exfiltration over USB

expected_logs.description: USB mass storage device connected (Samsung T7 SSD)
New drive letter E: mounted for removable device
Bulk file copy operations to removable drive E:\
Registry key modified: USBSTOR device history updated
xcopy.exe /E /H copying hidden and system files to USB

phases.name: Anti-Forensics and Track Covering
phases.description: The insider attempts to cover their tracks by deleting the staging directory, clearing recent file lists, emptying the Recycle Bin, clearing browser history, and using SDelete (Sysinternals) to securely wipe the staging area to prevent forensic recovery. They also modify file access timestamps using PowerShell to obscure the timeline. This systematic evidence destruction is a common final phase in insider threat cases documented by the CERT Insider Threat Center.

expected_logs.description: sdelete64.exe -p 3 -s securely wiping staging directory
Mass deletion of 340+ files from staging directory
PowerShell Set-ItemProperty -Name LastWriteTime to modify timestamps
RunDll32.exe InetCpl.cpl ClearMyTracksByProcess (IE/Edge history clear)
Windows Event Log cleared: Security log

expected_detection_points: File access to directories outside employee's normal scope (executive, engineering shares)
Bulk file access anomaly: 340+ files copied in single session
DLP alert: confidential files copied to local staging directory
7-Zip creating password-protected split archives with encrypted headers
Browser upload to personal cloud storage (Google Drive, Mega.nz)
Email with attachments sent to personal ProtonMail account
Unregistered USB mass storage device connected (Samsung T7)
Bulk file copy to removable media
PowerShell timestamp modification on recent files
